title: Lesson 0: Welcome to First Break AI

chapters.title: Roadmap walkthrough — Step 1: AI for coding

segments.text: Hey everyone. Welcome to First Break AI.
This is your first break in AI — a guided journey from commit to capstone.
There are six stages to this journey.
The first is: ship something real.
The second: see inside the machine.
Third: think at production scale.
Fourth: train your own model — this is where we'll spend maximum time of our cohort.
Fifth: ship a product. And sixth: prove it.
This cohort runs from 1 May 2026 to 30 June 2026 — around two months.
What are we expecting in this cohort?
Weekly office hours on Friday at 9:00 PM Indian Standard Time.
Regular blogs and content for you to follow to reach the end goal of this cohort.
Who is this cohort for?
You don't need any specific degree — you just need a starting point.
This cohort breaks the barriers that prevent people from moving into AI and becoming an expert.
It's for anyone who wants their first break into AI.
Student, professional, career switcher, or just simply curious — all welcome.
There is no prerequisite.
Follow the roadmap, build in the open, and let the work speak for itself.
Build a profile and let your work showcase what you've built.
To start the journey, join the Discord channel — the link is on the page.
To get started, look at the roadmap, the checklist, and complete the AI setup.
This cohort is free — you don't need expensive sign-ups.
Let's look at the roadmap, checklist, and AI setup one by one.
There are two ways to view the roadmap: a visual method, or a flat structure.
The first step is the first use of AI for coding.
The idea: set up a simple blog using any AI IDE.
Use Cursor or Claude Code to get this done.
First: understand GitHub basics.
Understand commit, pull request, and merge conflicts.
If you contribute to a project, there will be conflicts to resolve.
Setting up a personal blog is critical — somewhere to showcase your ideas and your journey.
The blog and code together will be the primary source of your work.
Apart from GitHub, we also need Hugging Face and a few other accounts.
Office hours are on Friday — summaries of every session will also be published.
We've already published four to five pre-cohort office hours.
Step two: run a model locally on your Windows, Mac, or Linux laptop.
See the nuts and bolts of a model first-hand.
You'll learn tokenization, chat templates, and basic inference.
Inference is where you run a model on infrastructure.
You'll understand model formats: GGUF vs SafeTensors.
Then the biggest part of any modern LLM: attention.
You'll learn concepts like KV cache.
Go through a few important papers and blogs.
The core: 'Attention Is All You Need'.
And blogs like The Illustrated Transformer by Jay Alammar.
There are also a few X / Twitter posts we'll discuss — what to expect from the LLM community and new LLM models in 2026.
The biggest theme being agentic models.
Step three is the inference deep dive — we'll pick this up a little later.
We'll move to step four — the core of this cohort.
You'll learn PyTorch fundamentals — PyTorch is used to train AI models.
You'll understand modeling: how a model looks, what the architecture is.
Data pipelines, fine-tuning, distributed training (DDP, FSDP), and more.
You can also go through The Journey.
The Journey is where you get a guided, voice-based narrative.
Right now, the first AI-based podcast is ready: 'Ship Something Real' — step one — paired with a Quarto blog.
This AI podcast was built using NotebookLM, Google's product.
We fed it transcripts from one of the pre-cohort office hours.
We expect better podcasts and quality in future sessions.
Still figuring out how to get NotebookLM to not over-exaggerate parts of the podcast.
On the podcast you can click any transcript line and it jumps to that point.
Quarto isn't WordPress — it's an open-source scientific and technical publishing system.
When you move to the second stage, the transcript appears alongside the audio.
On the Quarto site, you can see exploration of the steps.
Then we come to office hours — each session has a corresponding link to the roadmap.
The roadmap is your primary anchor — go back and see where you are in your journey.
Blogs, lessons, and everything else will all link back to the roadmap.
Example: the office hour on 10 April — we look at KV cache.
We look at models like Gemma 3 by Google, and basics of distributed data parallelism.
Plus concepts related to NVIDIA GPUs.
We also look at modeling — for example, RoPE in practice — with a great paper attached.
We'll also look at one small experiment called Speedrun — how to improve the architecture for a better model.
It's a toy project that helps you understand how training is done in practice.
Everything links back to the roadmap.
Then we have the checklist — pretty simple.
Number one: join the First Break AI Discord channel.
Then a few accounts: Hugging Face is required, GitHub is definitely required.
You can also sign in to Bubblspace AI — we're still pushing changes there.
A Google Colab free account is also useful.
Follow the cohort lead — my social handles are listed, and I'll follow back.
Then comes the AI setup.
Two things: GitHub setup, and an AI-based IDE — preferably Cursor, Claude Code, or Codex.
There are also other open-source tools — the choice is yours.
You'll need a ChatGPT account — start with the free tier.
OpenRouter — try to get those credits, they let you use models via API.
You can plug those models into your own custom app.
For GPU computing we'll primarily use Modal — a service provider for AI infrastructure.
Modal lets you deploy serverless GPUs, which you can use to train your model.
The first project will be Speedrun.
You get $5 in credits — and if you attach a credit card you can get up to $30. Pretty cool.
You can get a lot done with that $30.
We'll primarily use H100 GPUs, but look at alternative providers like E2E Networks (India-based) too.
Weights & Biases is critical — an experiment tracking tool that lets you see graphs while training runs.
We'll also push interesting projects in Project Watch — you can follow their journey.
Everything here is curated so it's useful for your learning.
So — this is the introduction to our cohort.
I hope this cohort is useful to you — expect more content in coming weeks.
The best idea is to build in public, and even contribute to this cohort.
All contributions are welcome. Welcome to First Break AI — and good luck!
